root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

classical mechanics IV: symplectic reduction

Table of contents

  1. Noether’s Theorem and symplectic reduction

Symmetries

Blah!

Definition. Let $\mfg$ be a Lie algebra, $V$ an $\bbr$-vector space, and $A$ a (possibly non-associative) $\bbr$-algebra.

  1. A linear representation of $\mfg$ on $V$, or a linear action of $\mfg$ on $V$, is a Lie algebra homomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \rho: \mfg \to \gl{V}. \end{equation}
  2. An action of $\mfg$ by derivations on $A$ is a Lie algebra homomorphism \begin{equation}\label{action-eqn} \rho: \mfg \to \Der{A}. \end{equation}

In particular, notice that an action by derivations is also a linear action, since we may compose the action map \eqref{action-eqn} with the inclusion map $\Der{A} \to \gl{A}$ which is a homomorphism of Lie algebras.

Theorem/Definition (Adjoint actions). Let $\mfg$ be a Lie algebra and for each $X\in \mfg$, define the $\bbr$-linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \ad_X : \mfg \to \mfg, \quad Y \mapsto \ad_XY = [X,Y]. \end{equation}

Then the map

\begin{equation}\notag \ad : \mfg \to \Der{\mfg}, \quad X\mapsto \ad_X, \end{equation}

is an action of $\mfg$ by derivations on itself. It is called the adjoint representation of $\mfg$.

Exercise. Prove the theorem.

Though the object in the next exercise might seem excessively formal, it will prove very important later.

Exercise. Let $\mfg$ be a Lie algebra with bracket $[-,-]$. Define a second Lie algebra, denoted $\mfg^\circ$, to be equal to $\mfg$ as an $\bbr$-vector space, but with bracket $[-,-]^\circ$ given by

\begin{equation}\notag [X,Y]^\circ = -[X,Y]. \end{equation}

Equipped with $[-,-]^\circ$, prove that $\mfg^\circ$ is a Lie algebra. It is called the opposite Lie algebra associated to $\mfg$.

Noether’s Theorem and symplectic reduction

The central result of this section is a version of Noether’s Theorem that identifies infinitesimal symmetries of a dynamical system (that leave the energy invariant) with conserved quantities. Though our machinery has been engineered in such a way to make the proof of this theorem a complete triviality, it is still one of the deepest theorems in physics.

We begin our discussion with infinitesimal symmetries realized as actions of Lie algebras. Of course, the most straightforward way to obtain such actions is by differentiating actions of Lie groups, which we will discuss later.

Definition. Let $M$ be a manifold, $(S,\omega)$ a symplectic manifold, $(P,\pi)$ a Poisson manifold, and $\mfg$ a (finite-dimensional, real) Lie algebra.

  1. An infinitesimal action of $\mfg$ on $M$ is a Lie algebra homomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \phi: \mfg \to \calx(M), \end{equation} where $\calx(M)$ is equipped with the usual Lie bracket of vector fields.
  2. An infinitesimal symplectic action of $\mfg$ on $S$ is a Lie algebra homomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \phi: \mfg \to \calx(S,\omega), \end{equation} where $\calx(S,\omega)$ is considered as a Lie subalgebra of $\calx(S)$.
  3. An infinitesimal Poisson action of $\mfg$ on $P$ is a Lie algebra homomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \phi: \mfg \to \calx(P,\pi), \end{equation} where $\calx(P,\pi)$ is considered as a Lie subalgebra of $\calx(P)$.
  4. An infinitesimal hamiltonian action of $\mfg$ on $P$ is a Poisson map \begin{equation}\notag \mu: P \to \mfg^\ast, \end{equation} where $\mfg^\ast$ is given its Lie-Poisson tensor making it a Poisson manifold.

In each of these four cases, we refer to elements $\xi\in \mfg$ as infinitesimal symmetries of the manifolds; in the fourth case, the mapping $\mu$ is called a momentum map.

The first three definitions all fit the same theme, while the definition of an infinitesimal hamiltonian action appears to be quite different from the first three. However, I claim that every hamiltonian action induces a Poisson action that factors through the Poisson gradient. To prove this, we first pass from $\mu$ to its pullback to obtain the algebra homomorphism

\begin{equation}\notag C^\infty(\mfg^\ast) \to C^\infty(P), \quad f \mapsto f \circ \mu. \end{equation}

If we pre-compose this map with the linear evaluation homomorphism

\begin{equation}\label{eval-eqn} \ev : \mfg \to C^\infty(\mfg^\ast), \end{equation}

then we obtain the map

\begin{equation}\label{comom-eqn} \mu^\ast: \mfg \to C^\infty(P), \quad \xi \mapsto \ev_{\xi} \circ \mu, \end{equation}

that we call the co-momentum map of the hamiltonian action. We observed in the previous section that the Lie-Poisson structure on $\mfg^\ast$ is the unique Poisson structure for which the evaluation map \eqref{eval-eqn} is a homomorphism of Lie algebras, and since $\mu$ is one too, we conclude that the co-momentum map $\mu^\ast$ is a homomorphism of Lie algebras. Since the manifold $P$ is Poisson, there is a Lie algebra homomorphism

\begin{equation}\notag C^\infty(P) \to \calx(P,\pi), \quad f\mapsto X_f, \end{equation}

which sends a function $f$ to its Poisson gradient. Composing the co-momentum map $\mu^\ast$ with this latter map yields a homomorphism of Lie algebras

\begin{equation}\notag \phi: \mfg \to \calx(P,\pi), \quad \xi \mapsto X_{\mu^\ast(\xi)}, \end{equation}

which gives the desired infinitesimal Poisson action of $\mfg$ on $P$.

Now, the main result of the section:

Noether’s Theorem. Let $J: \mfg \to C^\infty(P)$ be an infinitesimal hamiltonian action of a Lie algebra $\mfg$ on a symplectic manifold $(P,\omega)$, and let $H\in C^\infty(P)$ be a function. Then

\begin{equation}\notag \d H(X_{J(\xi)}) =0 \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \d(J(\xi))(X_H) =0 \end{equation}

for each $\xi \in\mfg$.

In view of the sequence of equalities

\begin{equation}\notag \d H(X_{J(\xi)}) =\left\{J(\xi),H\right\} = -\left\{H,J(\xi)\right\} = -\d(J(\xi))(X_H), \end{equation}

the desired equivalence is immediate. Q.E.D.

Though the vanishing conditions in Noether’s Theorem are symmetric, they have different physical interpretations: To say that

\begin{equation}\notag \d H(X_{J(\xi)}) =0 \end{equation}

means the energy $H$ is invariant under the symmetry $\xi$, while to say that

\begin{equation}\notag \d(J(\xi))(X_H) =0 \end{equation}

means the map $J(\xi)$ is conserved along the hamiltonian flow with energy $H$. This explains the name momentum mapping for $J$.

The symplectic manifolds of interest in classical dynamical systems are phase spaces, i.e., cotangent bundles. The following important result shows that any infinitesimal action on the configuration space of a system lifts to a hamiltonian action on phase space.

Theorem (Infinitesimal actions lift to hamiltonian actions). Let $\phi: \mfg \to \calx(Q)$ be an infinitesimal action of a Lie algebra $\mfg$ on a manifold $Q$, and define

\begin{equation}\notag J: \mfg \to C^\infty(T^\ast Q), \quad \xi \mapsto (\alpha_x \mapsto \alpha_x\left( (\xi_Q)_x\right)), \end{equation}

where $\xi_Q = \phi(\xi)$ for each $\xi\in \mfg$. Then $J$ is a well-defined homomorphism of Lie algebras, and hence an infinitesimal hamiltonian action of $\mfg$ on $T^\ast Q$. Furthermore, in canonical local coordinates $(q,p)$ on $T^\ast Q$, we have

\begin{equation}\label{yeah-eqn} J(\xi)(q,p) = p_i \xi^i_Q(q), \end{equation}

where $\xi^i_Q$ are the local components of the vector field $\xi_Q$.

I will leave the verification of the local formula \eqref{yeah-eqn} to the reader. Since the right-hand side of this equation clearly smoothly depends on $q$ and $p$, it follows that $J(\xi)$ is smooth, and hence the target of $J$ really is the space of smooth functions on the cotangent bundle. It follows from this local formula that

\begin{equation}\label{mom-par-eqn} \frac{\bd J(\xi)}{\bd q^j} = p_i \frac{ \bd \xi_Q^i}{\bd q^j} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\bd J(\xi)}{\bd p_j} = \xi^j_Q \end{equation}

for each $j=1,\ldots,n$.

Now, given two elements $\xi,\nu \in \mfg$, we shall prove that $J$ is a homomorphism of Lie algebras by verifying the equality of functions

\begin{equation}\label{target-eqn} J\left([\xi,\nu] \right) = \left\{ J(\xi) , J(\nu) \right\} \end{equation}

in the canonical local coordinates $(q,p)$. We first address the left-hand side, by computing

\begin{align} J\left( [\xi,\nu] \right)(q,p) &= (p_k dq^k|_q)\left([\xi_Q,\nu_Q]_q \right) \notag \\ &= \left( \xi_Q^j \frac{\bd\nu_Q^i}{\bd q^j} - \nu_Q^j \frac{\bd\xi_Q^i}{\bd q^j} \right)(p_k dq^k|_q )\left(\bd_{q^i}|_x \right) \notag \\ &= p_i \left( \xi_Q^j \frac{\bd\nu_Q^i}{\bd q^j} - \nu_Q^j \frac{\bd\xi_Q^i}{\bd q^j} \right), \label{fan1-eqn} \end{align}

where each function in the large set of parentheses is evaluated at $q$. Note that we used the fact $\phi$ is a homomorphism of Lie algebras in the first equality. Then, turning to the right-hand side of \eqref{target-eqn}, we compute

\begin{align} \left\{ J(\xi) , J(\nu) \right\}(q,p) &= \frac{\bd J(\xi)}{\bd p_j} \frac{ \bd J(\nu)}{\bd q^j} - \frac{ \bd J(\nu)}{\bd p_j}\frac{\bd J(\xi)}{\bd q^j} \notag \\ &= p_i \left( \xi_Q^j \frac{\bd\nu_Q^i}{\bd q^j} - \nu_Q^j \frac{\bd\xi_Q^i}{\bd q^j} \right), \label{fan2-eqn} \end{align}

with summation over $j$ implied on the right-hand side of the first equality, and where we’ve used the equations \eqref{mom-par-eqn} in the second equality. Our desired equality \eqref{target-eqn} then follows from the equality of \eqref{fan1-eqn} and \eqref{fan2-eqn}. Q.E.D.

As I mentioned at the beginning of this section, perhaps the most concrete method for producing infinitesimal actions is by differentiating actions by Lie groups. To demonstrate, let

\begin{equation}\notag M \times G \to M, \quad (x,g) \mapsto x\cdot g, \end{equation}

be a smooth right action of a Lie group $G$ on a manifold $M$. If $\mfg$ is the Lie algebra of $G$, then for each $\xi \in \mfg$ we have the exponential mapping

\begin{equation}\notag \exp: \bbr \to G, \quad t \mapsto \exp(t\xi). \end{equation}

The infinitesimal generator associated to $\xi$ is the smooth vector field $X^\xi\in \calx(M)$ with

\begin{equation}\notag X^\xi_x = \frac{\d}{\d t} \left(x\cdot \exp(t\xi)\right) \big|_{t=0} \end{equation}

for each $x\in M$. It is well known (see the section on Further Reading) that the mapping

\begin{equation}\notag \phi: \mfg \to \calx(M), \quad \xi \mapsto X^\xi, \end{equation}

is a homomorphism of Lie algebras, and thus an infinitesimal action of $\mfg$ on $M$.
